behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher behavior (recursive discovery and event emission across directory levels). A mocked watcher can hide platform/library misconfiguration where only root-level files trigger. Integration brake: (a) yes, requires real OS/filesystem events -> System. Rejected higher levels: behavior text contains no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent pipeline, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree. Create `root/sub/a.txt` after startup. Assert one pipeline execution for that file and output appears in the output directory without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: Deduplication discards a second processing request for the same path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is dominated by local time-window/path-key logic; real OS/network/subprocess are not required to reproduce the core failure. A fake clock and direct calls can expose off-by-one/window-reset bugs. Rejection gate: System is disqualified because behavior text is about request dedupe logic, not real filesystem event semantics; Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no model decisions are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedupe component/function directly with an injected clock: first request for `/x/f.txt` is accepted, second within window is rejected, third after window is accepted; verify path normalization expectations if applicable.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Could be Integration if dedupe state is only observable through daemon wiring, but the stated failure is still catchable at object/function boundary.

behaviors.description: Multiple filesystem events for one write result in exactly one processing run.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is specifically tied to OS-level duplicate event bursts for one write. In-process fakes often emit idealized single events and can miss real coalescing/debounce defects. Integration brake: (a) yes, real OS/filesystem event behavior needed -> System. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real watcher. Perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (create + append + rename or rapid successive writes). Assert pipeline invocation count is exactly one for that final file.

behaviors.description: If watched directory is removed and recreated during runtime, daemon resumes watching without restart.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real OS watcher lifecycle semantics (inode/path invalidation, handle reattachment after delete/recreate). Mocked watchers usually miss this edge. Integration brake: (a) yes, real filesystem/watcher behavior required -> System. Rejected higher levels: no model output dependency; no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on real temp watch dir. Delete the watch directory, recreate it with same path, then add a new file. Assert daemon recovers and processes new file without process restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive together, processing follows creation-timestamp order.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on interaction between watcher event timing, filesystem timestamp semantics/resolution, and queueing logic. A mock can hide real timestamp granularity/race behavior. Integration brake: (a) yes, relies on real filesystem metadata/event timing -> System. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML dependency and no agentic multi-step pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real watch directory, create several files nearly simultaneously with controlled timestamp differences where possible. Assert processed order matches observed creation timestamps (or defined tie-break rule when timestamps equal).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If implementation injects metadata and sorter is isolated, Integration could validate sorter logic, but this behavior wording targets real arrival conditions.

behaviors.description: A file still being written when arrival fires is processed only after writing completes.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem timing/locking/size-stabilization race. Stubs that instantly provide final content can always pass and hide partial-read failures. Integration brake: (a) yes, requires real OS/filesystem write-event behavior -> System. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML dependency and no agentic sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, begin a slow/streamed write to a file in watch dir, and keep descriptor open while writing chunks. Verify daemon does not process during partial state; after close/flush, verify single successful processing of full content.
